data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 155K Private Jobs Added In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The economic mover and shaker this week is Friday's employment report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in Total Nonfarm Employment.

The forecast for the forthcoming BLS report is that 139,000 jobs were added in March. However, each month a few days before we receive the highly anticipated jobs report, ADP releases their data on new nonfarm private jobs.

The ADP employment report revealed that 155,000 nonfarm private jobs were added in March, almost twice as much as the 84,000 from February. The latest reading was higher than the expected 118,000 addition.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report.

Here is an excerpt from today's ADP report press release:

"Despite policy uncertainty and downbeat consumers, the bottom line is this: The March topline number was a good one for the economy and employers of all sizes, if not necessarily all sectors."

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend. The latest six-month moving average is 171,000, down from 178,000 in February.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start.

The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into two categories: Goods Producing and Service Providing. The US is predominantly a services economy, so it comes as no surprise that services employment has shown stronger jobs growth.

It makes sense that service-producing employment has plummeted during the pandemic for a couple of reasons - our economy is mostly supported by service-producing jobs, and during the pandemic, those same services were brought to a halt. In March, goods-producing jobs rose by 24,000 while service-providing jobs added 132,000.

The sector with the largest growth in March was Professional and Business Services with a gain of 57,000. The biggest loser last month was Trade, Transportation, and Utilities with a loss of 6,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in March was the East North Central, with a gain of 76,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the Pacific region experienced the largest loss at -29,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into five sizes. Establishments with 500+ employees saw the largest growth in March, with a gain of 59,000 private jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

data.title: TCW MetWest Total Return Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review: The Economy – Breaking Down or Busting Out?

For an apparently fading economy that had sufficiently convinced the Federal Reserve to initiate monetary easing in September – with a 50 basis point (bp) cut, to boot – the market emphatically cast doubts on that view to close out 2024. Embracing the growth outlook, in an expression of dissent, broad equity indexes added to accumulated year-to-date gains while, more pertinently, interest rates sold off to move higher, especially out the yield curve. The summertime reflection that brought Fed Chair Powell to an ease was labor market weakness borne of decelerating job creation and rising unemployment, even as inflation remained sticky and above its 2% target. That rate “rebellion” started on cue from the September ease, with 5- to 10-Year U.S. Treasury yields up nearly 100 bps from mid-September to year-end, while the Fed eased two more times, delivering a “bear steepening” scenario. The bond market reaction was no doubt further abetted by an election outcome suggesting a more invigorated economy promising higher growth as virtue and potential tariff-induced inflationary effects as vice. Added up, though more of the rise DID come from the virtuous growth side of the ledger, it still meant for increased rates and generally disappointing bond returns as a result.

As a consequence of the higher rates, fixed income returns were negative as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index fell 3.1% in the fourth quarter and culminated a likewise unspectacular 1.3% advance for the year. While shorter maturity indexes delivered a flat Q4, i.e., no-to-limited losses, almost anything carrying duration beyond three years fell into the red and ceded earlier 2024 gains. Relatively, corporates captured a better experience for both Q4 and 2024 as higher coupons and resilient yield spreads generated considerable excess returns (to comparable duration U.S. Treasury issues) over those periods. While investment grade corporate outperformance was clear and durable, though still negative overall in Q4 due to the duration drag, high yield scratched out not only a marginally positive 0.2% Q4 return but a respectable 8.2% gain for the year also. (As an aside, this “risk on” sentiment was reflected in more buoyant equity market returns captured by the S&P 500’s 2.4% fourth quarter advance and 25% year-over-year gain, its second straight 20%-plus year, a feat last occurring some 20 years ago.) Among securitized issues, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) were also weighed down by higher rates to post a 3.2% Q4 loss, pulling the annual return down to a mere 1.2% gain, making it the laggard among the sectors. Non-agency MBS performed better for both the quarter and year against a backdrop of improving collateral fundamentals (mainly, falling loan-to-value ratios) and solid demand, while commercial MBS (CMBS) was similarly strong for the year with a 4.7% gain and good duration-adjusted returns, though lost 1.5% in Q4 given the rate moves. Asset-backed securities (ABS) provided the best returns among securitized products for 2024 with a 5% gain, owing largely to the floating rate issuance providing insulation from rate volatility and offering attractive coupons.

Performance

Challenged by a higher rate environment, the TCW MetWest Total Return Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) fell 3.60% net of fees in the final quarter of 2024 to trail the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (“Index”) by 54 bps, while the full year return of 1.05% was 20 bps behind the Index. The main drag on relative performance in both periods was the Fund’s above-Index duration throughout the year, which was extended alongside the rise in rates given our view that current rates remain restrictive, exceed equilibrium conditions, and are poised to settle lower. Mitigating the duration drag was an overweight to front-end maturities as the curve steepened out (and longer maturities lagged) over the year. From an allocation perspective, relative returns were dampened by an ongoing underweight to a corporate credit sector that continued to trade inordinately rich in the TCW team’s view. While this underweight acted as a headwind, firm pricing provided opportunities to trim into strength and optimize existing holdings, leading to issue selection benefits within industries like cable-satellite, airlines, and non-cyclicals that outperformed the broader Index. Countering the corporate underweight was an overweight across securitized sectors, with agency MBS constituting the largest position. The overweight to agency MBS weighed on performance during Q4 given elevated rate volatility, though was beneficial for the year as yield premiums fell and buying returned from banks and overseas investors. Yield premiums also fell across non-agency MBS to benefit Fund performance in both periods while also contributing attractive income. Meanwhile, with CMBS and ABS among the best performing securitized sectors, the overweights to each benefitted relative returns, while additional contributions came from issue selection, led by single asset single borrower (SASB) CMBS deals and CLOs within ABS – both of which helped the Fund carry an overall yield advantage relative the Index.

Outlook & Positioning

Though the dominant theme across markets has been the re-emergence of a “Goldilocks” economy following the election and attendant reinvigoration of U.S. growth prospects, there remains potential for volatility that could challenge market conditions and pricing. For one, the labor market continues to cool and reflect sagging employment demand which, despite not yet flowing through to higher unemployment, has been evident in more leading indicators. Despite being seemingly lost among the recent market moves, TCW remains of the view that the labor market is the key factor driving the direction of the economy, and that the trend of weak at worst and mixed-at-best data portends challenges to economic growth potential. Second, valuations, most notably credit spreads, have almost never been tighter, meaning considerable good news is already embedded in prices, creating an interesting paradox in that there is little to no protection should things not unfold as markets expect – which is of course exactly when one would want that protection. In terms of those expectations, market pricing in November clearly indicated that investors project great things from the new administration…and immediately, judging from risk market pricing. However, policy implementation takes time, and any potential benefits are likely to be felt with meaningful lags and with potential volatile secondary effects that may be difficult to fully account for today. Lastly, the recent surge in Treasury yields has kept rates restrictive, i.e., above equilibrium, and at levels that are likely to come down over the intermediate and longer term due to slowing effects throughout the real economy, most notably in higher financing costs that weigh on economic growth and, of course, on those most in need of credit.

TCW remains committed to our time-tested value-oriented philosophy, with bottom-up portfolio construction and relative value sector allocations the pillars by which we introduce carry and return potential to portfolios. This is reflected in a significant and broad underweight to corporate credit given tight credit spreads, though with targeted positions in high-conviction issuers and industries like communications and utilities, the latter offering a variety of opportunities to capitalize on the structural tailwind of power generation and demand. Meanwhile, securitized products present a more compelling relative value proposition given wider yield spreads, informing a widespread overweight across sectors. Agency MBS remains the largest position given favorable liquidity, the government guarantee, and over 50 bps of spread premium versus investment grade credit. Though non-agency MBS doesn’t carry the government guarantee, the sector is bolstered by robust collateral profiles that help maximize cashflow back to bondholders, with the ongoing evolution of structures and deal types across the sector fertile ground to capture alpha potential at the issue level. Similarly, CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, facilitating bottom-up inclusion criteria. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations like senior CLO tranches and high-quality non-traditional collateral types like single-family rentals and data centers. Finally, the duration position ended the year approximately 0.8 years long versus the Index, with an overweight to the 2- and 5-Year parts of the yield curve given expectations for lower rates and a steeper curve.

Investment Risks

It is important to note that the Fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. Government. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk, the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk, and price volatility risk. Funds investing in bonds can lose their value as interest rates rise and an investor can lose principal. Mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities often involve risks that are different from or more acute than risks associated with other types of debt instruments. MBS related to floating rate loans may exhibit greater price volatility than a fixed rate obligation of similar credit quality. With respect to non-agency MBS, there are no direct or indirect government or agency guarantees of payments in pools created by non-governmental issuers. Non-agency MBS are also not subject to the same underwriting requirements for the underlying mortgages that are applicable to those mortgage-related securities that have a government or government-sponsored entity guarantee. For a complete list of Fund risks, please see the Prospectus.

Glossary of Terms

Agency MBS– The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities– A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (BPs)– One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg”– Is a broad- based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond– A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations) – A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral – Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit– A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation’s debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Coupon– The interest rate stated on a bond when it’s issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. Federal Reserve (the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. Floating Rate– Any interest rate that changes on a periodic basis. The change is usually tied to movement of an outside indicator, such as the prime interest rate. High Yield– A bond that is rated below investment grade. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade– A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody’s, Standard & Poor's and Fitch. Labor Market– The nominal market in which workers find paying work, employers find willing workers, and wage rates are determined. Liquidity– The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. MBS(Mortgage-Backed Securities) – A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by a accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Non-agency – Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS– Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Non-cyclical– Non-cyclical stocks repeatedly outperform the market when economic growth slows. Non-cyclical securities are generally profitable regardless of economic trends because they produce or distribute goods and services we always need, including things like food, power, water, and gas. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. S&P 500- The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor’s 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Securitized– When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Total Return– The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Tranches– Are segments created from a pool of securities—usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages—that are divvied up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. Treasury Yield– The return on investment, expressed as a percentage, on the debt obligations of the U.S. government. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. U.S. Treasuries (U.S. Treasury Securities)– Bills, notes and bonds that are debt obligations of the U.S. government. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve – A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity.

data.title: The 10.5% Yield Of The Preferred Stock Of Chimera Is Attractive In This Painful Market
data.description:
Almost two years ago, I recommended buying the preferred stock of Chimera (NYSE:CIM.PR.D) for its 10.0% dividend yield and the wide margin of safety of the dividend. Since that article, the preferred stock has offered an outstanding total return of 43% and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500, which has gained 36% over the same period. The return of the preferred stock is impressive, particularly given the lower risk of fixed-income securities when compared to common stocks.

About four months ago, I lowered my rating from "buy" to "hold" but stated that the stock was still fairly attractive. Since that article, the preferred stock has outperformed the S&P 500, as it has offered a total return of -2% whereas the S&P 500 has declined 7%. The big question is whether investors should hold the stock after a long period of outperformance or the time has come to take profits, particularly given the deteriorating economic landscape.

Business overview

I mentioned the properties of the preferred stock of Chimera in the second article mentioned above. Chimera has $1.6 billion of common stock and $0.93 billion of preferred stock, for a total equity capital of approximately $2.5 billion. At its par value of $25, the preferred stock has a fixed to floating yield of 5.64% plus 3-months SOFR. As the latter is currently standing at 4.36%, the yield of the preferred stock at its par value is 10.0%. Even better, the preferred stock is now trading at $23.70, i.e., at a 5.2% discount to its par value. As a result, its current yield is 10.5%.

Unfortunately, the economic landscape has deteriorated lately for Chimera due to the policy of the new government. The implementation of excessive tariffs on many imported goods is likely to cause a material increase in the prices of these goods. While the effect of tariffs on inflation is not visible yet, it will probably be visible soon, as inflation is greatly affected by the expectations of companies and employees. When companies expect high inflation, they raise their prices proactively. At the same time, employees demand higher wages in anticipation of inflation. To cut a long story short, inflation is not likely to decrease meaningfully anytime soon.

The aggressive rate-hiking policy of the Fed drove inflation from a 40-year high of 9.1% in 2022 to 2.4% in September 2024. As a result, the Fed began reducing interest rates in that month. Unfortunately, the healthy trend of a decreasing inflation has stalled since then, with inflation now standing at 2.8%. As the risk of a resurgence of inflation has increased, the Fed has paused its rate-cutting cycle. Even worse, it may have to raise interest rates again in the future if inflation rises above 3.0%.

Such a development will be negative for Chimera. The company profits from the difference between long-term interest rates and short-term interest rates. Therefore, a surge of short-term interest rates exerts a strong headwind to its results. This is clearly reflected in the excessive loss per share of -$7.53 in 2022, when the Fed boosted interest rates drastically in an effort to put inflation under control. The loss in 2022 is 59% of the current market capitalization of the stock.

On the bright side for the preferred shareholders of Chimera, the yield of the preferred stock will rise in tandem with interest rates in such an adverse scenario. As a result, the preferred stock will offer some protection against inflation. As long as inflation does not increase too much, the floating yield of the preferred stock is likely to reward the shareholders adequately.

The other risk related to the current business landscape is the possibility of an upcoming recession. If the prices of many goods rise too much, consumers may drastically reduce their spending. As consumption constitutes approximately 70% of GDP, the risk of a recession has increased. That's why Goldman Sachs raised the odds of an upcoming recession from 20% to 35% a few days ago.

A potential recession will probably result in an inverted yield curve, which will exert pressure on the profit margin of Chimera. In addition, defaults on mortgages are likely to increase in such a scenario. Therefore, the increased probability of an upcoming recession is a risk factor for Chimera.

However, such a negative scenario will weigh much more on the common stock of Chimera than on the preferred stock. The preferred dividend has a wide margin of safety, as it cannot be cut unless the common dividend is eliminated first. Notably, Chimera has cut its common dividend in some instances due to its volatile results, but it has never suspended its common dividend. Even in the Great Recession, the worst mortgage crisis of the last 90 years, Chimera continued paying a common dividend. The same happened during the coronavirus crisis in 2020 and the severe downturn caused by the surge of inflation and interest rates in 2022. Overall, Chimera has never suspended its common dividend since it became public, in 2007. This record certainly bodes well for the safety of the preferred dividend.

Recent business performance

In the fourth quarter, Chimera grew its operating earnings per share 3% sequentially, from $0.36 to $0.37, as it reduced its provisions for loan losses. Its average cost of funds was 4.5%, but its average yield on earning assets stood at 6.0%, thus resulting in a net interest spread of 1.5%. In the last 12 months, the company has posted net income of $176 million and has paid $104 million in preferred dividends. It thus has a preferred dividend payout ratio of 56%, which reflects a decent margin of safety.

Moreover, the company posted a healthy loan-to-value [LTV] ratio of 43% in the fourth quarter.

Source: Investor Presentation

This means that the remaining value of the mortgages of the company are only 43% of the current value of the properties, thus providing a significant margin of safety to the mortgage REIT. The positive trend in this metric is depicted in the chart below.

Source: Investor Presentation

Since 2019, the LTV ratio of Chimera has greatly improved, from 66% to 43%. It is also remarkable that Chimera has $1.5 billion of interest rate swaps and hence it has hedged 69% of its floating rate secured recourse financing. In other words, the company is somewhat protected from unexpected moves in interest rates over the next 12 months. Nevertheless, investors should not conclude that Chimera is resilient to unexpected moves in interest rates.

The bottom line

The stock market offered annual returns of approximately 25% for two consecutive years but this year has been off to a rough start due to the tariff-related policy of the new government, which has increased the risk of high inflation and a slowdown or a recession in the economy. In this tumultuous and painful market, the 10.5% preferred dividend of Chimera is fairly attractive, particularly given its wide margin of safety. The preferred stock is also offering some protection against a resurgence of inflation thanks to its floating yield. Nevertheless, given the weak economic outlook, the sensitivity of the results of Chimera to the status of the economy and the volatile performance record of Chimera, I maintain my hold rating on the stock.

data.title: TCW Global Real Estate Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The TCW Global Real Estate Fund, I Share Class, (the “Fund”) generated a return of -11.75% (net of fees) during the quarter, underperforming the S&P Global REIT Index gain of -8.78%. Our overweight exposure to Hotels, Resorts, & Cruise Lines contributed positively while the overweight in Industrial REITs and Telecom Tower REITs detracted most from relative performance.

Investment Environment

The fourth quarter proved to be volatile for equities, specifically in the U.S., although the S&P 500 ultimately rose 2.4% (Total Return) in the quarter (in USD terms). U.S. stocks surged through the end of November as investors digested the Republican sweep in Washington, only to fade in December after the Federal Reserve (Fed) updated its economic projections for 2025 with a more tepid view of rate cuts. Futures are currently reflecting ~1 cut in 2025 of 26 basis points (bps), down from the prior view of ~3 cuts totaling 72 bps. Despite the elevated interest rate environment over the last few years, the economy has continued to advance. Consumers have felt the pressure, as evidenced by increasing delinquencies, although unemployment remains historically low and spending has been stable. GDP growth has continued to advance and corporate earnings’ growth has remained in the high single-digits, with expectations for low double-digit growth in each of the next two years.

Real Estate stocks generally struggled compared to the broader market as inflation has remained stubbornly persistent and the tone towards interest rates has turned more hawkish. Geographically, U.S. stocks tended to outperform their international peers in the quarter due to solid earnings growth.

Against this backdrop, the performance of Global REIT indices saw pronounced weakness in the quarter. The RMZ (MSCI U.S. REIT Index) was down 6.1% for the quarter (+8.8% YTD). In Japan, the TSE REIT Index was down 3.1% for the quarter (-4.1% YTD), underperforming the Nikkei 225, which was up 5.3% for the quarter (+21.3% YTD). Globally, the S&P Global REIT Index fell 8.7% (+4.0% YTD) vs. a loss of 0.6% (+18.9% YTD) for the S&P Global 1200.

Sector Weightings

During Q4-24, the top performing sectors in the S&P Global REIT Index was Data Center REITs (+7.82%), no other sector produced a positive return in the period. The worst performing sectors were Self-Storage REITs (-17.73%) and Industrial REITs (-16.66%). Our portfolio was most overweight in Telecom Tower REITs and Real Estate Services, and most underweight in Retail REITs and Health Care REITs. From a sector allocation standpoint, our Fund benefitted most from its overweight to Hotels, Resorts, & Cruise Lines. The Fund’s overweight in Industrial REITs and its overweight in Telecom Tower REITs detracted the most from performance.

Security Selection*

Our best performing securities for the quarter were Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR; 4.81%**; Data Center REITs) and Wyndham Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (WH; 1.83%**, Hotels, Resorts, & Cruise Lines).

DLR operates data center facilities, leases the space out to tenants, and provides the power, cooling, and connectivity solutions needed to operate their IT infrastructure. Secular drivers such as global data and compute proliferation (inclusive of what we are seeing in the AI industry), enterprises increasingly outsourcing data center needs, a rising value on interconnection, and the globalization of the world economy all set up promising demand trends for DLR. The company is seeing unprecedented levels of bookings and high pricing in the wake of historically low vacancy rates in the industry. Of the few public data center companies that are available to investors, DLR provides the best mix of exposure to the recent acceleration of large scale leasing on the back of AI and to the more predictable stream of leasing from traditional enterprise leasing as the economy becomes more digital.

WH is the world’s largest hotel franchising company by number of hotels, with 9,200 affiliated worldwide. Wyndham tilts further towards the economy and midscale segment than its publicly-traded peers, which tend to focus on upscale and above. The company operates 25 brands, including Days Inn, Howard Johnson, Ramada, and Super 8. The Wyndham Rewards loyalty program has over 110 million members. Wyndham offers an attractive, asset-light business model and generates the majority of its revenue through franchise and management fees. Wyndham consistently grows its hotel network by mid/high single digits each year through a combination of new hotel development (financed by the hotel owner, not WH) and conversions (typically an independent hotel looking to reduce their reliance on OTAs). The company also grows revenues through improvements in occupancy and average room rate, though these are more economically sensitive.

Our two worst performing securities were Innovative Industrial Properties Inc.†(IIPR; Industrial REITs) and Americold Realty Trust, Inc. (COLD; 3.33%**; Industrial REITs).

IIPR is one of the largest real estate providers to the regulated cannabis industry. The company engages in triple-net leases to cannabis operators in which IIPR develops the property for use and effectively all operating expense items thereafter are passed through to the tenant. The cannabis industry, from top-to-bottom, has seen increased volatility in recent months, with many stocks selling off after the U.S. election. IIPR sold off again in December after the company reported that a key tenant defaulted on rent obligations for multiple leases. We opted to close the position and reallocate funds elsewhere in the portfolio.

COLD operates temperature-controlled warehouses, which are an integral piece of the food supply-chain, with COLD being one of two largest players in the space. The company can offer an integrated network of storage and distribution assets that are more complex to run than traditional warehouse assets. This allows for pricing power on top of the secular drivers that come from global population growth, income growth, and mix shift towards perishable foods (via customer perception shift towards fresh food). Additionally, the company benefits from consolidation of what is a very fragmented industry, as well as an ability to layer technology onto these traditionally mom-and-pop assets to generate efficiency gains. The industry is currently undergoing duress in the form of a weakening low-income consumer combined with higher wage expenses. Structurally, this should allow for consolidation opportunity and a strengthened business for COLD longer term.

Representative Buys (Ticker; Sector)*

SBA Communications Corp (SBAC; 3.92%**; Telecom Tower REITs) operates a predominantly U.S. network of macro cell towers, though with solid exposure in select international markets such as South America, Central America, Canada, and South Africa. Secular drivers such as increased data traffic domestically, increased wireless penetration internationally, and the upcoming tailwind from 5G (only ~50% of the of the coverage phase has been deployed, with high-value densification phase to follow) should result in promising organic growth prospects for the foreseeable future. Management anticipates 6% to 7% growth rates for SBAC, which should translate into 70%+ EBITDA margins through via a high operating leverage model.

Lennar Corp (LEN; 2.75%**; Industrial REITs) is one of the largest homebuilders in the U.S. with a focus on entry level and move up buyers. Lennar has structural demand tailwinds. After the Great Financial Crisis, the supply of new homes has declined so there is a supply & demand imbalance that favors homebuilders. Lennar benefits from first-time household formation with its entry-level homes in attractive markets.

Shurgard Self Storage Limited (SHUR BB or BQZCBZ (OTCPK:SSSAF); 2.69%**; Self-Storage REITs) operates a portfolio of self-storage facilities across various European countries, including the Netherlands, France, Sweden, United Kingdom, Belgium, Germany, and Denmark. SHUR has a robust project development pipeline scheduled through the next ~5 years, valued over €1 billion and equal to approximately 26% of its existing rental portfolio. Management expects nearly €100 million of additional NOI to flow through the company as a result of the projects. The company plans to have an additional 365k rental spaces available by the end of 2026.

Representative Sells (Ticker; Sector)†*

During the quarter we exited Innovative Industrials Proper Inc. (IIPR; Industrial REITs), Weyehauser Company (WY; Timber REITs), and D.R. Horton, Inc. (DHI; Homebuilding).

Market Outlook

Looking forward, against the above stated macroeconomic backdrop, the fundamentals for the equity market generally remain solid. Per Bloomberg consensus expectations, the U.S. economy is expected to grow at a 2.7% rate this year, and corporate EPS growth is expected to accelerate from around 9% in 2024 to about 13% in 2025. We’re mindful though that after back-to-back annual gains of 25%+, stock market valuations are stretched with the forward P/E ratio currently at nearly 22 times. Interestingly, UBS notes (1/2/25) that after the Magnificent 7 names (“Big 6 Tech+”) trounced the rest of the U.S. stock market last year (48% vs.16%), those same market leaders this year are poised to grow earnings per share 19% vs. 11% for the other names. We think that the trajectory for U.S. equities this year will in large part be driven by the ability of these market leaders to sustain strong earnings growth and, at the same time, by the extent to which the broader market delivers on heightened EPS growth expectations. We fully expect the uncertainty associated with the yet-to-be-defined policy initiatives of the Trump Administration to result in substantial market volatility, and we are keeping a watchful eye on the labor market (continuing jobless claims reached a three-year high in December) and potential signs of any consumer weakness beyond the well-documented struggle of low-income consumers.

Against this backdrop, we remain largely constructive on the real estate sector. While certain areas (e.g. Office and Retail) may continue to experience demand weakness, other areas (e.g. Data Centers) are currently thriving. Furthermore, we believe that with the Fed still on a monetary loosening campaign, the path of least resistance for interest rates is likely lower (albeit with some volatility until inflation becomes more contained). We believe that this sets up real estate and REITs with tailwinds, and we look for the space to begin making up some ground on relative performance.

We value your support and thank you for your trust.

Iman H. Brivanlou, PhD, Lead Portfolio Manager, Managing Director – High Income Equities

Nehal Patel, Lead Analyst, Vice President – High Income Equities
